analogy: Think of "The Human Eye and the Colourful World" through the chapter's own opening images: Electricity 11 CHAPTER Electricity has an important place in modern society. It is a controllable and convenient form of energy for a variety of uses in homes, schools, hospitals, industries and so on. What are the factors that control or regulate the current through an... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 11 CHAPTER, 1 A = 1 C/1 s. Small quantities of current are expressed in milliampere, Q U E S T I O N S, 1 An electric cell. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
The Human Eye and the Colourful World is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that electricity 11 CHAPTER Electricity has an important place in modern society.

The text explains that it is a controllable and convenient form of energy for a variety of uses in homes, schools, hospitals, industries and so on.

The text explains that what are the factors that control or regulate the current through an electric circuit?

The text explains that in this Chapter, we shall attempt to answer such questions.

The text explains that we shall also discuss the heating effect of electric current and its applications.

The text explains that 11.1 ELECTRIC CURRENT AND CIRCUIT 11.1 ELECTRIC CURRENT AND CIRCUIT 11.1 ELECTRIC CURRENT AND CIRCUIT 11.1 ELECTRIC CURRENT AND CIRCUIT 11.1 ELECTRIC CURRENT AND CIRCUIT We are familiar with air current and water current.

The text explains that we know that flowing water constitute water current in rivers.

The text explains that similarly, if the electric charge flows through a conductor (for example, through a metallic wire), we say that there is an electric current in the conductor.

The text explains that in a torch, we know that the cells (or a battery, when placed in proper order) provide flow of charges or an electric current through the torch bulb to glow.

The text explains that we have also seen that the torch gives light only when its switch is on.

The text explains that a switch makes a conducting link between the cell and the bulb.

The text explains that a continuous and closed path of an electric current is called an electric circuit.

The text explains that now, if the circuit is broken anywhere (or the switch of the torch is turned off ), the current stops flowing and the bulb does not glow.

The text explains that electric current is expressed by the amount of charge flowing through a particular area in unit time.

The text explains that in other words, it is the rate of flow of electric charges.

The text explains that in circuits using metallic wires, electrons constitute the flow of charges.

The text explains that so, electric current was considered to be the flow of positive charges and the direction of flow of positive charges was taken to be the direction of electric current.

The text explains that conventionally, in an electric circuit the direction of electric current is taken as opposite to the direction of the flow of electrons, which are negative charges.

The text explains that if one end of the tube is connected to a tank of water kept at a higher level, such that there is a pressure difference between the two ends of the tube, water flows out of the other end of the tube.

The text explains that for flow of charges in a conducting metallic wire, the gravity, of course, has no role to play; the electrons move only if there is a difference of electric pressure – called the potential difference – along the conductor.

The text explains that (Nichrome is an alloy of nickel, chromium, manganese, and iron metals.) n First use only one cell as the source in the circuit.

The text explains that the potential difference, V, across the ends of a given metallic wire in an electric circuit is directly proportional to the current flowing through it, provided its temperature remains the same.

The text explains that (11.4), R is a constant for the given metallic wire at a given temperature and is called its resistance.

The text explains that a component used to regulate current without changing the voltage source is called variable resistance.

The text explains that precise measurements have shown that resistance of a uniform metallic conductor is directly proportional to its length (l ) and inversely proportional to the area of cross-section (A).

The text explains that both the resistance and resistivity of a material vary with temperature.

The text explains that alloys do not oxidise (burn) readily at high temperatures.

The text explains that for this reason, they are commonly used in electrical heating devices, like electric iron, toasters etc.

The text explains that example 11.3 (a) How much current will an electric bulb draw from a 220 V source, if the resistance of the bulb filament is 1200 Ω?

The text explains that (b) How much current will an electric heater coil draw from a 220 V source, if the resistance of the heater coil is 100 Ω?

The text explains that note the difference of current drawn by an electric bulb and electric heater from the same 220 V source!

The text explains that science 180 Example 11.4 The potential difference between the terminals of an electric heater is 60 V when it draws a current of 4 A from the source.

The text explains that if the diameter of the wire is 0.3 mm, what will be the resistivity of the metal at that temperature?

The text explains that will current flow more easily through a thick wire or a thin wire of the same material, when connected to the same source?

The text explains that 11.6 RESIST 11.6 RESIST 11.6 RESIST 11.6 RESIST 11.6 RESISTANCE OF A SYSTEM OF RESISTORS ANCE OF A SYSTEM OF RESISTORS ANCE OF A SYSTEM OF RESISTORS ANCE OF A SYSTEM OF RESISTORS ANCE OF A SYSTEM OF RESISTORS In preceding sections, we learnt about some simple electric circuits.

The text explains that redraw the circuit of Question 1, putting in an ammeter to measure the current through the resistors and a voltmeter to measure the potential difference across the 12 Ω resistor.

The text explains that science 188 11.7 HEA 11.7 HEA 11.7 HEA 11.7 HEA 11.7 HEATING EFFECT OF ELECTRIC CURRENT TING EFFECT OF ELECTRIC CURRENT TING EFFECT OF ELECTRIC CURRENT TING EFFECT OF ELECTRIC CURRENT TING EFFECT OF ELECTRIC CURRENT We know that a battery or a cell is a source of electrical energy.

The text explains that the chemical reaction within the cell generates the potential difference between its two terminals that sets the electrons in motion to flow the current through a resistor or a system of resistors connected to the battery.

The text explains that we have also seen, in Section 11.2, that to maintain the current, the source has to keep expending its energy.

The text explains that a part of the source energy in maintaining the current may be consumed into useful work (like in rotating the blades of an electric fan).

The text explains that rest of the source energy may be expended in heat to raise the temperature of gadget.

The text explains that on the other hand, if the electric circuit is purely resistive, that is, a configuration of resistors only connected to a battery; the source energy continually gets dissipated entirely in the form of heat.

The text explains that therefore, the source must supply energy equal to VQ in time t.

The text explains that hence the power input to the circuit by the source is P V VI = Q t = (11.19) Or the energy supplied to the circuit by the source in time t is P × t, that is, VIt.

The text explains that what happens to this energy expended by the source?

The text explains that an electric lamp of 100 Ω, a toaster of resistance 50 Ω, and a water filter of resistance 500 Ω are connected in parallel to a 220 V source.

The text explains that what is the resistance of an electric iron connected to the same source that takes as much current as all three appliances, and what is the current through it?

The text explains that the law implies that heat produced in a resistor is (i) directly proportional to the square of current for a given resistance, (ii) directly proportional to resistance for a given current, and (iii) directly proportional to the time for which the current flows through the resistor.

The text explains that in practical situations, when an electric appliance is connected to a known voltage source, Eq.

The text explains that in electric circuits, the unavoidable heating can increase the temperature of the components and alter their properties.

The text explains that two lamps, one rated 100 W at 220 V, and the other 60 W at 220 V, are connected in parallel to electric mains supply.

The text explains that what current is drawn from the line if the supply voltage is 220 V?

Source: NCERT Class 10 Science, Chapter 11: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
